serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one plate or bowl of steamed apple snails with lemongrass), providing roughly 280 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Snails, raw (FDC ID 175178) and lemongrass seasoning components; adapted for Vietnamese steamed preparation with fish sauce dipping sauce
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese lemongrass-steamed apple snail exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central snail (raw) data (FDC ID 175178: protein ~16.1 g, fat ~1.4 g, carbohydrate ~2.7 g per 100 g raw) adjusted for steaming (minimal nutrient loss) and the addition of fish sauce dipping sauce (contributing sodium and minor carbohydrate). Input record values (protein 10 g, fat 2.67 g, carbohydrate 6.67 g, sodium 300 mg per 100 g) are plausible for a prepared dish including sauce components and are retained as the best available estimate. Energy recalculated as (10 × 4) + (2.67 × 9) + (6.67 × 4) = 40 + 24.03 + 26.68 = 90.71 kcal ≈ 93 kcal, consistent with input. Values used with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on shellfish/mollusc category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese lemongrass-steamed apple snail. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous shellfish and mollusc foods in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show low GI values due to high protein content, low carbohydrate, and minimal starch. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (30 × 20.01) / 100 ≈ 6.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce dipping sauce (300 mg/100 g reported; actual may be higher)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced or omitted fish sauce, as the reported 300 mg sodium per 100 g (equivalent to ~900 mg per 300 g serving) approaches one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and actual sodium may be underestimated due to fish sauce variability.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~30), high protein (10 g/100 g), moderate carbohydrate (6.67 g/100 g), low sugar (2.67 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index, high protein content supporting satiety and glycaemic control, and low sugar load; however, fish sauce in the dipping sauce may contain added sugar and should be checked, and pairing with high-GI accompaniments such as white rice should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.67 g/100 g), low trans fat (0.0 g/100 g), moderate cholesterol (33.33 mg/100 g), high protein, steamed preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The steamed preparation method avoids added fats, saturated fat is very low at 0.67 g/100 g, and trans fat is absent, making this dish broadly suitable for cardiovascular health; sodium from fish sauce remains the primary concern and portion control is advised to keep cumulative sodium intake within recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (lemongrass, ginger), very low saturated fat, steamed preparation avoids AGE formation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steaming avoids advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation; lemongrass and ginger provide anti-inflammatory compounds; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should request reduced chilli, and fish sauce quantity should be minimised to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ốc Bươu Hấp Sả (Apple Snail Steamed with Lemongrass) is a popular Vietnamese seafood dish featuring apple snails (Ốc Bươu, Pomacea canaliculata or Pila polita) steamed over or with fresh lemongrass stalks, ginger, and aromatic herbs. The snails are typically served in their shells with a dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, and sugar. The dish is widely consumed as a street food and casual restaurant dish across Vietnam and in Vietnamese communities in Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The steaming method preserves the natural sweetness and firm, slightly chewy texture of the snail meat while infusing it with the citrusy, floral aroma of lemongrass. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 93 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 10 g, low fat at 2.67 g (saturated fat only 0.67 g), and moderate carbohydrate at 6.67 g, primarily from the dipping sauce components. Sodium is approximately 300 mg per 100 g, largely attributable to fish sauce, and may be higher depending on preparation. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated ~30) making it suitable for glycaemic management. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease management due to its low GI, low saturated fat, and high protein; sodium management is the primary concern for hypertension. Lemongrass and ginger contribute anti-inflammatory phytochemicals relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) based on shellfish/mollusc category from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this dish. Country of origin in input record was listed as Singapore, which likely reflects the data collection context (GrabFood Singapore); the dish is of Vietnamese culinary origin and region_of_origin is set to Vietnam accordingly.
